hoses: Hydrant supply hose from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Monitor Supply Line A: 300 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
Monitor Supply Line B: 300 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
nozzles: Portable monitor with 1⅜-inch smooth bore tip, 500 GPM @ 80 PSI
appliances: Portable monitor positioned uphill from Engine 181
sceneElements.elevation: Portable monitor is 60 ft above Engine 181, using elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft

problems.title: Flow Per Monitor Supply Line
problems.question: The elevated portable monitor is flowing 500 GPM total and is supplied by two equal hose lines from Engine 181. The two lines split the monitor flow; they do not each flow the full monitor total. What flow should be used for each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Two equal supply lines split the flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a two-line monitor supply, calculate friction loss per line using the flow in each line, not the total monitor flow in each line.
problems.explainMistake: Do not put 500 GPM in each line. The two supply lines split the 500 GPM monitor flow.

problems.title: Friction Loss Per Supply Line
problems.question: Each elevated-monitor supply line is 300 ft of 2½-inch hose. The monitor flows 500 GPM total through two equal lines, so calculate each line at one-half of the total flow. Using FL = C × (GPM/100)² × (length/100), with C = 2, what is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each supply line carries 250 GPM. At 250 GPM through 300 ft of 2½-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 40 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the total 500 GPM to calculate friction loss for one line. The total flow is split between the two supply lines.

problems.title: Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The portable monitor is positioned 60 ft above Engine 181. At 0.5 PSI per foot of elevation gain, what elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = elevation height × 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: Because the monitor is uphill from Engine 181, elevation pressure must be added. A 60 ft elevation gain requires 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract elevation pressure for an uphill operation. Uphill hose lays require additional pressure.

problems.title: Portable Monitor Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying an uphill portable monitor with two equal 300 ft 2½-inch supply lines. The monitor flows 500 GPM total; split the flow equally between the two lines for friction-loss calculations. The monitor uses 80 PSI nozzle pressure, has 25 PSI monitor appliance loss, and is 60 ft above the pump. Use elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 40 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For the portable monitor operation, calculate each supply line separately. Since both lines are equal, either line gives the same friction loss. Add friction loss, monitor nozzle pressure, elevation, and appliance loss for a final pump discharge pressure of 175 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add friction loss for both supply lines together. The two equal supply lines split the flow, and the pump discharge pressure is based on the pressure required by one supply path.

problems.title: Hydrant Supply Line Count
problems.question: The operation has one hydrant supply line connected to Engine 181’s intake and two separate discharge lines supplying the portable monitor. For water-supply tracking, count only intake-side hydrant supply lines feeding Engine 181. How many hydrant supply lines feed Engine 181?
formulaBreakdown: The scene shows one hydrant supply hose connected to Engine 181.
problems.instructorExplanation: Only one hydrant supply line is connected from the hydrant to Engine 181. The two blue hoses are monitor supply lines from Engine 181 to the portable monitor.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the two blue monitor supply hoses as hydrant supply lines. They are discharge lines going uphill to the monitor.
